questions.question_markdown: Evaluate $\int_{-2}^{-1} \frac{d x}{\sqrt{-x^{2}-6 x}}$.
questions.solution_markdown:
The key is to notice that the integrand can be rewritten as:

$$
\frac{\frac{1}{3}}{\sqrt{1-\left(\left(\frac{x-3}{3}\right)^{2}\right.}}
$$

Then, we have that the antiderivative of $\arcsin (u)$ where $u=(x-3) / 3$.

questions.solution_markdown: Recall $a_{0}=f(c)$. Hence, we must find the roots of $f$. Note that the roots must divide 32 because the polynomial is monic.

questions.question_markdown: Suppose that $X$ and $Y$ are independent random variables with standard deviations $\sigma_{X}$ and $\sigma_{Y}$, respectively. If $Z=X+Y$, then $\sigma_{Z}=$
questions.solution_markdown:
Since $X$ and $Y$ are independent the variance is additive:

$$
\sigma_{Z}^{2}=\operatorname{Var}(X+Y)=\operatorname{Var}(X)+\operatorname{Var}(Y)=\sigma_{X}^{2}+\sigma_{Y}^{2}
$$

questions.question_markdown:
The function

$$
f(x, y)= \begin{cases}\frac{x^{2} y}{x^{4}+y^{2}} & (x, y) \neq(0,0) \\ 0 & (x, y)=(0,0)\end{cases}
$$

Which of the following is true?

(I) $f$ is not continuous at $(0,0)$.

(II) $f$ is differentiable everywhere

(III) $f$ as a well defined partial derivatives everywhere (i.e. $\frac{\partial f}{\partial x}, \frac{\partial f}{\partial y}$ are both defined)

(IV) $f$ is continuous at $(0,0)$ but not differentiable at $(0,0)$.
questions.solution_markdown: I can be shown to be true by looking at the function along $y=x^{2}$. Along this curve, $f=1 / 2$. III is true away from the origin and is true at the origin to since $f=0$ along the $x$ - and $y$-axes.

questions.question_markdown:
Let $T$ be a linear operator on $\mathbb{R}^{3}$ equipped with the standard inner product. Which of the following must be true?

(I) If $W$ is subspace such that $T(W) \subset W$, then $W^{\perp}=\{v \in V \mid\langle w, v\rangle=0, v \in W\}$ also has the property that $T\left(W^{\perp}\right) \subset W^{\perp}$.

(II) If $T$ is self-adjoint, then $T^{-1}$ exists and is diagonalizable.

(III) If $T=\left(\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 1 & -1 \\ 1 & 5 & 3 \\ -1 & 3 & -1\end{array}\right)$, then $\mathbb{R}^{3}$ has an orthonormal basis of eigenvectors for $T$.

(IV) If $T^{2}$ is diagonalizable, so is $T$.
questions.solution_markdown: I is false when $T=\left(\begin{array}{ll}1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1\end{array}\right)$. II is false because the zero matrix is diagonalizable so $T^{-1}$ doesn't exist. III is true by the spectral theorem. IV is false as $\left(\begin{array}{ll}0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0\end{array}\right)$ (this matrix is in Jordan form; but you could also check that it's not diagonalizable by hand).

questions.question_markdown: Evaluate: $\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{1}{1+\tan x} d x$ (TRICKS INVOLVED!)
questions.solution_markdown:
The first trick is to rewrite ths integral as:

$$
I_{1}=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\cos x}{\sin x+\sin x} d x
$$

Next consider

$$
I_{2}=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sin x}{\sin x+\sin x} d x
$$

Add and subtract these two integrals from each other to get integrals you can actually do. For instance,

$$
I_{2}-I_{1}=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sin x-\cos x}{\sin x+\sin x} d x=\left.\ln (\sin x+\cos x)\right|_{x=0} ^{\pi / 2}=0
$$

Easily, $I_{1}+I_{2}=\pi / 2$. From here we see $2 I_{2}=\pi / 2$ and then get our answer.

questions.question_markdown:
Which of the following are true of a continuous function $f: \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ such that $f(x+1)=f(x)$ for all $x$.

(I) $f$ achieves it's maximum and minimum

(II) $f$ is uniformly continuous.

(III) There is a $x_{0}$ such that $f\left(x_{0}+\pi\right)=f\left(x_{0}\right)$

(IV) There is a $x_{1}$ such that $f\left(x_{1}+\frac{1}{2}\right)=f\left(x_{1}\right)$.
questions.solution_markdown:
Restricting f to $[0,1]$, we see that $f$ must attain its min and max. We want to apply the theorem that a continuous function on a compact domain is uniformly continuous. We can't just use $[0,1]$ because we don't know how .99 and .01 compare. So, we just enlarge our space of consideration, namely to $[0,2]$ and apply the theorem.

To get III and IV, use the auxiliary function $g(x)=f(x)-f(x+\pi)$. This is continuous and since $f$ attains maximum and minimum values, call them $x$ and $y$, respectively. then $g(x) \geq 0$ and $g(y) \leq 0$. To finish, apply the IVT.

questions.question_markdown: Let $S$ be a set with $|S| \geq 3$. How many non-surjective functions from $S \rightarrow\{1,2,3\}$ are there?
questions.solution_markdown:
You could let $|S|=3$ and count the number of non-surjective functions, which is 21 . B is the only answer choice that works. Alternatively, you could note that what we want to do is first choose 2 elements of the 3 that will be "hit" and then count the number of functions into a two element. However, we have overcounted by 3 the number of functions in which all elements are sent to a single element in $\{1,2,3\}$.

$$
\binom{3}{2} 2^{|S|}-3
$$

Alternatively, use the inclusion-exclusion principle:

$$
2^{|S|}-\left(2^{|S|}-\binom{3}{1} 2^{|S|-1}+3\right)
$$

questions.question_markdown:
Let $X$ and $Y$ be random variables. Which of the following is always true?

(I) $E(X+Y)=E(X)+E(Y)$

(II) $\operatorname{Var}(X)=\operatorname{Var}(-X)$

(III) $P(\max (X, Y)<2)=P(X<2) P(Y<2)$

(IV) $\operatorname{var}(X+Y)>\operatorname{Var}(X)+\operatorname{Var}(Y)$
questions.solution_markdown: It's a good idea to be able to go through the definitions and prove I and II. Also, III is false, but true when $X, Y$ are independent because if $\max (X, Y)<2$, then both $X<2$ AND $Y<2$. Then apply the definition of independence. However, if $X=Y$, then $\max (X, Y)=X$ and the statement is false. IV is false because if $Y=-X$ where $X$ is your favorite Random Variable (work this out!).

(II) $f_{2}:(0,1) \rightarrow[0,1]$ continuous and surjective

(III) $f_{3}:(0,1) \rightarrow[0,1]$ continuous and bijective

questions.question_markdown: The angle between the tangent plane $x^{2}+y^{2}+2 z^{2}=4$ at ( $1,1,1$ ) and the $x y$-plane is:
questions.solution_markdown: It amounts to finding the angle between the two normals of the planes, namely ( $2,2,4$ ) and $(0,0,1)$. This is done by the formula $\theta=\arccos \left(\frac{(2,2,4) \cdot(0,0,1)}{\|(2,2,4)\| \cdot\|(0,0,1)\|}\right)$

questions.question_markdown:
Which of the following are subrings of polynomials with coefficients in $\mathbb{Z}$ ?

(I) Even degree polynomials.

(II) Polynomials of degree less than or equal to 3 .

(III) Polynomials all of whose coefficients are even.
questions.solution_markdown:
We have to verify the following properties:

Proposition. Let $S$ be a commutative ring, and let $R$ be a subset of $S$. Then $R$ is a subring of $S$ if and only if:

(i) $R$ is closed under addition and multiplication

(ii) if $a \in R$, then $-a \in R$ (this implies that we have the additive identity in $S$ )

(iii) $R$ contains the multiplicative identity of $S$.

Note that I fails (i) because $x^{2}+x$ and $x^{2}$ are both even degree but their difference is odd degree. II fails (ii) because $x^{3} x^{3}$ has degree bigger than 3 . III fails (iii) because the identity has an odd coefficients. III is an ideal, though, meaning it satisfies the first two properties, but not the last.

questions.question_markdown:
For how many values of the parameter $k$, will the following system have no solutions?

$$
\begin{array}{r}k x+y+z=1 \\ x+k y+z=k \\ x+y+k z=k^{2}\end{array}
$$
questions.solution_markdown: The left is linear and the determinant in terms of $k$ should be $(k+2)(k-1)^{2}$. For $k=1$, there are infinitely many solutions, while for $k=2$ there none since adding the last two equations yields $2 x-y-z=-2$, which is inconsistent with the first. Lots of computations here!

questions.question_markdown: Evaluate $\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{1}{1+(\tan x)^{e}} d x$
questions.solution_markdown:
Similar to the previous $\# 10$. Rewrite the integral as:

$$
I_{1}=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\cos ^{e} x}{\sin ^{e} x+\sin ^{e} x} d x
$$

Use the substitution $u=\pi / 2-x$. Then note this integral becomes:

$$
I_{2}=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sin ^{e} x}{\sin ^{e} x+\sin ^{e} x} d x
$$

Hence, $I_{1}=I_{2}$. So, we find $I_{1}+I_{2}=\pi / 4=2 I_{1}$. We could have replaced the $e$ with any real number!!!

questions.question_markdown:
Which of the following are compact sets?

(I) $G L(2, \mathbb{R}) \subset \mathbb{R}^{4}$, the set of invertible $2 \times 2$ matrices over $\mathbb{R}$.

(II) $S L(2, \mathbb{R}) \subset \mathbb{R}^{4}$, the set of $2 \times 2$ matrices over $\mathbb{R}$ with determinant 1 .

(III) $O(2, \mathbb{R}) \subset \mathbb{R}^{4}$, the set of orthonormal $2 \times 2$ matrices over $\mathbb{R}$
questions.solution_markdown:
III is closed and bounded. Bounded is easy because each of the columns have norm less than 1 (might want to expand what it means to be bounded and make sure you can prove this). Closed is straight forward because if $A$ is an orthonormal matrix with columns $\vec{x}, \vec{y}$, then consider the continuous function $g(A)=\langle\vec{x}, \vec{y}\rangle$. Using properties of continuity, we see that if $\left\{A_{n}\right\}$ is some sequence of orthogonal matrices with limit $B$, then $B$ must have orthogonal columns. Similar, arguments work for the norm of each column.

To see that I and II are not compact use:

$$
A_{n}=\left(\begin{array}{ll}1 & n \\ 0 & 1\end{array}\right)
$$

questions.question_markdown: Which set does not have the same cardinality as the others?
options: $\{$ solutions to polynomial equations with coefficients in $\mathbb{Z}\}$.
questions.solution_markdown:
Some relevant facts:

  -  The finite cartesian product of countable sets is countable.
  -  Similarly, a finite cartesian product set of infinite sets each of the same cardinality has the same cardinality.
  -  The countable union of countable sets is countable.
  -  The countable cartesian product of countable is uncountable (Canter's diagonal argument)

All the sets are uncountable except for (d), which is the countable union of countable sets.

questions.question_markdown:
Let $G$ be a finite group. Which of the following never happen?

(I) A subgroup of $G$ with index 2 that is not normal.

(II) All the conjugacy classes of $G$ have size 1 .

(III) G has no proper subgroups other than the subgroup containing only the identity.
questions.solution_markdown: A subgroup of index 2 is always normal. Let $H$ be a subgroup of index 2 . The left- and right-multiples of H partition $G$. A set of index two means there are only two subgroups in each partition: $\{H, a H\}$ and $\{H, H a\}$. Hence, $H a=a H$ and $H$. Hence $I$ cannot happen. II happens when $G$ is abelian. III happens when $G=\mathbb{Z} / 7 \mathbb{Z}$, say.

questions.question_markdown:
Let $(x(t), y(t))$ be a parametric curve in $\mathbb{R}^{2}$ be given by:

$$
\begin{aligned}x(t) & =e^{t} \\ y(t) & =\sin (t)\end{aligned}
$$

What is $\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}$ as a function of $t$ ?
questions.solution_markdown:
Lots of different ways. One way is to use the chain rule:

$$
\frac{d^{2} y}{d t^{2}}=\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}\left(\frac{d x}{d t}\right)^{2}+\frac{d y}{d x} \frac{d^{2} x}{d t^{2}}
$$

and we know $\frac{d y}{d x}=\left(\frac{d y}{d t}\right) / \frac{d y}{d t}$. So:

$$
\Longrightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{\frac{d^{2} y}{d t^{2}}-\frac{d y}{d x} \frac{d^{2} x}{d t^{2}}}{\left(\frac{d x}{d t}\right)^{2}}
$$

and

$$
\frac{-\sin t-\cos t}{e^{2 t}}
$$

questions.question_markdown:
Which of the following are true about subgroups of $(\mathbb{Q},+)$

(I) If a subgroup is finitely generated, then it is cyclic.

(II) There non-cyclic subgroups.

(III) There are no finite subgroups other than the trivial one.
questions.solution_markdown: Write everything over a common denominator say $n$ and note any subgroup $H$ looks like $H=\frac{1}{n} \cdot \widetilde{H}$, where $\widetilde{H}$ is a subgroup of $\mathbb{Z}$. Since $\mathbb{Z}$ is a PID (aka every subgroup is cyclic), we have $\frac{m}{n} \mathbb{Z}=\widetilde{H}$. II is true as $G=\left\{m / 2^{n}: m, n \in \mathbb{Z}\right\}$. III is true since every element has infinite order.

questions.question_markdown: A tank contains 50 gal of pure water. Starting at time zero, a salt-water solution containing 0.4 $\mathrm{lb} / \mathrm{gal}$ of salt enters the tank at a rate of $2 \mathrm{gal} / \mathrm{min}$. At the same time a drain is opened at the bottom of the tank allowing the salt solution to leave at a rate of $2 \mathrm{gal} / \mathrm{min}$. Assume that the solution is kept perfectly mixed at all times. How long will it take for the amount of salt (in lbs) in the tank to reach 10 lbs ? Leave your answer in exact form (in terms of logarithms).
questions.solution_markdown:
$\mathrm{D} y^{\prime}=.4 \cdot 2-\frac{y}{50} \cdot 2 \Longrightarrow S^{\prime}+y / 25=.8$. Hence the solution can be solved by an integration factor $e^{t / 25}$ and you get $20+C e^{-t / 25}$. Since $S(0)=20$, we have:

$$
y=20-20 e^{-t / 25}
$$

Setting $y=10$ and solving gives the answer.

questions.question_markdown: Evaluate $\int_{0}^{2 \pi} e^{e^{i \theta}} d \theta$.
questions.solution_markdown:
Pretty hard, but if you have taken complex analysis, you might think this looks a lot like $\int_{|z|=1} \frac{e^{z}}{z} d z$ and hence by the Cauchy Integral Formula is $2 \pi i e^{0}$. The key is to draw the picture in the complex plane and then figure out a complex integral for it. The parameterization $\phi(\theta)=e^{i \theta}$, so $d z=i e^{i \theta} d \theta$ and:

$$
\int_{|z|=1} \frac{e^{z}}{z} d z=\int_{0}^{2 \pi} \frac{e^{e^{i \theta}}}{e^{i \theta}}\left(i e^{i \theta} d \theta\right)
$$

questions.question_markdown:
Let $f: \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ be differentiable, then:

$$
\lim _{\varepsilon \rightarrow 0} \frac{3}{\varepsilon^{3}} \int_{-\varepsilon}^{\varepsilon} t f(x+t) d t=
$$
questions.solution_markdown:
One can use taylor expansion of $f(x+t)$ around $x$ or simply apply $f(u)=x$, then:

$$
\begin{aligned}\lim _{\varepsilon \rightarrow 0} \frac{3}{\varepsilon^{3}} \int_{-\varepsilon}^{\varepsilon} t(x+t) d t & =\lim _{\varepsilon \rightarrow 0} \frac{3}{\varepsilon^{3}} \int_{-\varepsilon}^{\varepsilon} x t^{2}+t d t \\ & =\lim _{\varepsilon \rightarrow 0} \frac{3}{\varepsilon^{3}}\left[\frac{x t^{3}}{3}+\left.\frac{t^{2}}{2}\right|_{-\varepsilon} ^{\varepsilon}\right] \\ & =2 x\end{aligned}
$$

Well, $f^{\prime}(x)=x$ and so the answer should be $2 f^{\prime}(x)$.

questions.question_markdown: Let $A=\left(\begin{array}{ll}1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1\end{array}\right)$ and define $\|A\|_{o p}:=\sup _{\|v\|_{\mathbb{R}^{2}}=1}\left\{\|A v\|_{\mathbb{R}^{2}}\right\}$. There is a vector $v$ such that $\|A\|_{o p}= \|A v\|_{\mathbb{R}^{2}}$. Also, note that once we find one $v$ that maximizes the norm $\|*\|_{o p},-v$ also does. That is $\tan 2 \theta=2$.

questions.question_markdown: Let $F(x)=\int_{\sin x}^{\cos x} e^{t^{2}+x t} d t . F^{\prime}(0)=$
questions.solution_markdown:
Set $G(a, b, c)=\int_{b}^{a} e^{t^{2}+c t} d t$. Then $F(x)=G(\cos x, \sin x, x)$ and apply the chain rule to get:

$$
F^{\prime}(x)=e^{\cos ^{2} x+x \cos x}(-\sin x)-e^{\sin ^{2} x+x \sin x}(\cos x)+\int_{\sin x}^{\cos x} t e^{t^{2}+x t} d t
$$

and

$$
F^{\prime}(0)=-1+\int_{0}^{1} t e^{t^{2}}=-1+\frac{e}{2}-\frac{1}{2}=\frac{1}{2}(e-3)
$$

questions.question_markdown:
Which of the following holmorphic functions must be constant functions:

(I) An entire function with positive real part.

(II) An entire function whose image does not intersect a line $L \subset \mathbb{C}$.

(III) An entire function such that $f(0)=0$ and $f(z) / z \rightarrow 0$ as $|z| \rightarrow \infty$. (Hint: $f(z) / z$ has a removeable singularity at 0 , so can be extended to an entire function).

(IV) A holomorphic function whose range misses two points.
questions.solution_markdown:
For I, an entire function with positive real part mean that $e^{-f}$ is bounded. For II, by adjusting $f$ so that $L$ is imaginary axis, i.e. $\alpha f+\beta$, we can assume the image of $f$ has positive real part or negative real part as in I.

Could also use Picard's theorem, which says that entire functions can only miss at most a single point in their image (for instance $e^{z}$ misses 0 ).

For III, by the maximum modulus principle we know that $|f(z) / z|$ takes its maximum on the boundary of disk. Couple this that its getting closer to zero shows $f(z) / z=0$. Hence, $f(z)=0$. IV is easily nonconstant because we don't assume the function is entire. If it was, apply Picard's theorem.

questions.question_markdown: How many times do the hands of a analog clock (i.e. one with a hour and minute hand) form perpendicular angles in a 24 hour time cycle?
questions.solution_markdown:
The key to this problem is picking the right "reference frame". Let's look at the frame of the hour hand, i.e. we are sitting there and watch the minute hand go around. Every revolution in this reference frame corresponds to two right angles. In a 12-hour period, the clock makes one revolution and the minute hand makes 12 , but end up at the same original positions. That means 11 revolutions with respect to our special reference frame were made. Thus, 22 right angles in a 12 -hour period. In a 24 hour period, 44 angles are obtained.

Even more formally, let $\theta(t):=$ the total radians the minute hand travels in the reference frame $t$ hours after 12 o'clock. Specifically, $\theta(0)=0$. To count the revolutions in this reference frame is to calculate $\theta(24) / 2 \pi$. We note that $d \theta / d t=m$, where $m$ is constant (this follows since both minute and hour hand move at constant rates). So, $\theta(t)=m t$. Now, $m$ must be calculated. Well, $\theta(1)=(11 / 12) \cdot 2 \pi=11 \pi / 6=m$ (this is one o'clock). So,

$$
\begin{aligned}& \theta(24)=m t=\frac{11 \pi}{6} \cdot 24=44 \pi \\ & \frac{\theta(24)}{2 \pi}=22\end{aligned}
$$

The number of right angles is thus 44 , which we came up with.

questions.question_markdown:
Let $R$ be the rectangle bounded by $x-y=0, x-y=2, x+y=0$ and $x+y=3$. Evaluate

$$
\iint_{R}(x+y) e^{x^{2}-y^{2}} d A
$$
questions.solution_markdown:
This is a change of variables:

$$
\int_{U} f(\phi(\vec{x}))=\int_{\phi^{-1}(U)} f(\vec{y}) \det\left(D \phi^{-1}\right)
$$

where $\phi: \mathbb{R}^{2} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^{2}$ is a diffeomorphism. $\phi(x, y)=(x+y, x-y)=(u, v)$ and then our integrand becomes $u e^{u v}$. We see that $\det(D \phi)=2$, so $\det\left(D\left(\phi^{-1}\right)\right)=\det\left((D \phi)^{-1}\right)=\frac{1}{2}$ and the integral becomes:

$$
\frac{1}{2} \int_{0}^{3} \int_{0}^{2} u e^{u v} d v d u=\frac{1}{2} \int_{0}^{3}\left(e^{2 u}-1\right)=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{e^{6}}{2}-\frac{1}{2}-3\right)
$$

questions.question_markdown:
Which of the following statements are false?

(I) The set of holomorphic functions are dense in the set of continuous on $D=\left\{\vec{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{2} \| \vec{x} \mid \leq 1\right\}$ functions with respect to the metric $d(f, g)=\sup _{\vec{x} \in D}|f(\vec{x})-g(\vec{x})|$.

(II) Let $\left\{f_{n}\right\}$ be a sequence of functions on the unit disk $\{z:|z| \leq 1\}$ in the complex plane. If the sequence of functions converge pointwise, then they also converges uniformly.

(III) Let $\left\{f_{n}\right\}$ be a sequence of infinitely differentiable functions on $[0,1]$. If there is a function $g$ such that $f_{n}^{\prime} \rightarrow g$ uniformly and $f_{n}(0)=1$, then there is a function $G$ such that $G(0)=1$ and $f_{n} \rightarrow G$ uniformly.
questions.solution_markdown: Morera's theorem implies the "I" if false because if holomorphic functions converge to another continuous function under this metric (i.e. converge uniformly), then the continuous function must have integral zero (uniform convergence implies limits and integration commute). Clearly, there are continuous functions that have non-zero path integral. II is false ( $f_{n}(x)=x^{n}$ ). It is false even when it converges to a a continuous function! (take the disappearing hump). III is true simply by writing out the fundamental theorem of caluculus. The complete details can be found for instance in Strichartz, Way of Analysis pg. 262 or Tao, Analysis II pg. 464

questions.solution_markdown: I is false whe $A=\left(\begin{array}{cc}0 & -1 \\ 1 & 1\end{array}\right)$. II is true by multiplicativity of the determinant. III is false when $A=\left(\begin{array}{cc}1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1\end{array}\right)$.

questions.question_markdown:
Consider the intial value problem:

$$
\left\{\begin{array}{l}y^{\prime}=y^{1 / 2} \\ y(0)=a\end{array}\right.
$$

where $a$ is a constant. Which of the following is true?

(I) If $a=0$, the unique solution to the above is $y(t)=0$.

(II) If $a=0$, there are exactly 2 solutions.

(III) If $a=0$, there are infinitely many solutions.

(IV) If $a \neq 0$, there is a unique solution.
questions.solution_markdown:
The relevant theorem is:

Theorem. If $y^{\prime}=f(t, y)$ is differential equation with initial condition $y\left(t_{0}\right)=y_{0}$, then there exists a solution a solution if $f$ is continuous in the ty-plane in a neighborhood of $\left(t_{0}, y_{0}\right)$. Uniqueness is obtained if $f$ is lipschitz in the $y$ variable in a neighborhood of $\left(t_{0}, y_{0}\right)$ (easier to just check if $\frac{\partial f}{\partial y}$ is continuous).

Let's suppose $a=0$. Note that $y=0$ and $y=\frac{t^{2}}{4}$ is a solution. We can actually amalgmate these solutions. For $C \geq 0$ we have solutions:

$$
y_{C}(t)= \begin{cases}0 & x \leq C \\ \frac{1}{4}(x-C)^{2} & x \geq C\end{cases}
$$

However, for $a \neq 0$, the theorem above holds.

questions.question_markdown: Let $C_{1}, C_{2}$ be the circles of radius 1 centered at ( 1,0 ) and ( 0,1 ) respectively. Find the area on which the interiors of the circles overlap.
questions.solution_markdown:
Use polar coordinates. The first circle is $r=2 \cos \theta$ and the second is $r=2 \sin \theta$. The area is then easiest split in half from $\theta=\pi / 4$ to $\pi / 2$ and from $\pi / 4$ to $\pi / 2$. These have the same area by symmetry:

$$
2 \int_{\pi / 4}^{\pi / 2} \int_{0}^{2 \cos \theta}(r d r d \theta)=-\int_{\pi / 4}^{\pi / 2} 4 \cos ^{2} \theta d \theta=\left.(2+\sin 2 x)\right|_{x=\pi / 4} ^{\pi / 2}=\pi / 2-1
$$

Yet another way (thanks Devin), is to use basic geometry and symmetry. Essentially cut out a quarter circle with the square. Key point is the circles intersect at $(1,1)$

A square matrix is said to be nilpotent if $A^{n}=0$ for some $n \in \mathbb{N}$. Which of the following are true?

questions.question_markdown:
Which of the following sets are dense in the set of square $n \times n$ square matrices over $\mathbb{C}$ ?

(II) Unitary Matrices Matrices

(III) Symmetric Matrices

(IV) Diagonalizable Matrices
questions.solution_markdown: Remember we view $n \times n$ matrices as $\mathbb{R}^{n^{2}}$. We use that all complex matrices have a basis in which they are upper triangular. Perturb the diagonal entries so that they are either nonzero (I) or all distinct (II). The others are not true. For II, if $\det A \neq 1$, then there is an open set $U \subset \mathbb{R}$ such taht $\det A \in U$ and $1 \notin U$. Hence, $\det^{-1}(U)$ is an open set which excludes unitary matrices around $A$. Symmetric matrices are clearly not dense.

questions.solution_markdown: If $f(x)=\sqrt[3]{x}^{3}$, then $f$ is not additive. If $f=0$, then $f(1)=0$, too. C is true because $f$ is linear and hence must be surjective. To see this, $f(m+n)=(m+n) f(1)=m f(1)+n f(1)=f(m+n)$ when $m, n \in \mathbb{Z}$. Then for rational points, assume a common denominator: $f(m / n+p / n)= \frac{1}{n} \cdot f(m+p)=\frac{1}{n} f(m)+\frac{1}{n} f(p)=f(m / n)+f(p / n)$. Since $f$ is continuous, this extends to all real numbers by taking limits.

questions.question_markdown:
Which of the following are subspaces?

(I) $\left\{\left(x_{1}, x_{2}, x_{3}\right) \in \mathbb{C}^{3}: x_{1}+i x_{2}+3 x_{3}=0\right\}$

(II) $\left\{\left(x_{1}, x_{2}, x_{3}\right) \in \mathbb{C}^{3}: x_{1}+2 x_{2}+3 x_{3}=4-5 i\right\}$

(III) $\left\{\left(x_{1}, x_{2}, x_{3}\right) \in \mathbb{C}^{3}: x_{1} x_{2} x_{3}=0\right\}$

(IV) $\left\{\left(x_{1}, x_{2}, x_{3}\right) \in \mathbb{C}^{3}: x_{1}=10 x_{3}\right\}$
questions.solution_markdown:
Use:

Proposition. Let $V$ be a vector space over a field $F$. A subset $U \subset V$ is a subspace if the following conditions are satisfied:

(a) (Additive Identity) $\overrightarrow{0} \in U$

(b) (Closure under Addition) $\vec{u}, \vec{v} \in U$ implies $\vec{u}+\vec{v} \in U$

(c) (Closed under scalar multiplication) $a \in F$ and $\vec{u} \in U$ implies $a \vec{u} \in U$.

questions.question_markdown:
If $S$ is a ring with the property that $s=s^{2}$ for each $s \in S$, which of the following must be true?

(I) $s+s=0$ for each $s \in S$

(II) $(s+t)^{2}=s^{2}+t^{2}$ for each $s, t \in S$

(III) $S$ is commutative (i.e. multiplication is commutative)
questions.solution_markdown:
Note the ring must have characteristic 2 because if we look at the homomorphism $\varphi: \mathbb{Z} \hookrightarrow S$ that sends $1_{\mathbb{Z}}$ to $1_{S}$, we know that $\left(1_{S}+1_{S}\right)=\left(1_{S}+1_{S}\right)^{2}$. That means that $2 \in \operatorname{ker} \varphi$. This easily tells us I. II and III are intimately related because:

$$
s^{2}+t s+s t+t^{2}=(s+t)^{2}=s+t=s^{2}+t^{2}
$$

hence, $s t=-t s$, but we have characteristic 2 . Hence, II and III are true.

questions.question_markdown:
For each positive integer $n$, let $f_{n}$ be the function defined on $[0,1]$ by

$$
f_{n}(x)=\frac{x^{n}}{1+x^{n}}
$$

Which of the following statements are true?

(I) The sequence $\left\{f_{n}\right\}$ converges pointwise to a limit function $f$.

(II) The sequence $\left\{f_{n}\right\}$ converges uniformly to a limit function $f$.

(III) $\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \int_{0}^{1} f_{n}(x) d x=\int_{0}^{1}\left(\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} f_{n}(x)\right) d x$.
questions.solution_markdown:
We see that $f_{n} \rightarrow 0$ on $[0,1)$ and $1 / 2$ for $x=1$. Therefore II must be false. III is true inspite of II because:

$$

questions.question_markdown:
Suppose $X$ is a compact and $Y$ is Hausdorff such that $f: X \rightarrow Y$ is a continuous bijection. Which of the following are true?

Closed subsets of compact spaces are compact (open covers union the complement). Need to show that $f$ is open. Can show it's closed (not true in general, but since $f$ is bijective we can do this). See the website:

\href{http://www.proofwiki.org/wiki/Continuous_Bijection_from_Compact}{http://www.proofwiki.org/wiki/Continuous\_Bijection\_from\_Compact}

\_to\_Hausdorff\_is\_Homeomorphism

questions.question_markdown:
Which of the following are equivalent to continuity?

(I) $f^{-1}\left(A^{o}\right)=\left(f^{-1}(A)\right)^{o}$ where $S^{o}$ denotes interior of a set $S$

(II) $f^{-1}(\bar{A})=\overline{\left(f^{-1}(A)\right)}$

(III) $f^{-1}\left(A^{c}\right)=\left(f^{-1}(A)\right)^{c}$
questions.solution_markdown: Let's show why I fails. If $f: \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ is given by $x \mapsto x^{2}$ and $A=[0,1]$, then $f^{-1}\left(A^{0}\right)= f^{-1}((0,1))=(-1,0) \cup(0,1)$, whereas $\left(f^{-1}(A)\right)^{0}=(-1,1)$. For II, let $f:[-1,2]$ be given by $f(x)=x^{2}$. When $A=(1,4)$, then $f^{-1}(\bar{A})=\{1\} \cup[1,2]$, wherease $\overline{f^{-1}(A)}=[1,2]$. III is false because it holds for ALL functions, not just continuous ones.

questions.question_markdown:
A circular region is divided by 5 radii into five equal area sectors. Twenty one points are chosen in the circular region none along the circular radii that divide each region. Which of the following statements are true?

(I) Some sector contains at least 5 points

(II) Some sector contains at most 3 points

(III) Some pair of adjacent sectors contains a total of at least 9 of the points.
questions.solution_markdown: Direct pigeon hole application for I. For II, place all points in one region to see fallacy. For III. Suppose $n$ is the number of balls in one sector, then $8-n$ are the balls in adjacent sectors and the remaining balls in the two adjacent sectors not accounted for is $21-2(8-n)-n=5+n$. If we choose $n \geq 5$ (and obviously smaller than 9 ), then the remaining two places can have 9 points.

questions.question_markdown: From the set of integers $\{1,2, \ldots, 9\}$, how many nonempty subset sum to an even integer?
questions.solution_markdown:
There are two ways to do this. The first is to directly count by noticing that we have to have an even number of odds chosen:

$$
2^{4}\left(\binom{5}{0}+\binom{5}{2}+\binom{5}{4}\right)-1
$$

where we have omitted the emptyset. Alternatively, we can show there is a bijection between sets that sum to even and odd numbers simply by mapping a set to another by removing or adding 1 to the set depending on whether 1 is already a member. This shows the bijection, but since this maps $\{1\} \rightarrow \emptyset$.

questions.question_markdown: Let $y=f(x)$ be a solution of the differential equation $x d y+\left(y-x e^{x}\right) d x=0$ such that $y=0$ when $x=1$. What is $f(2)$ ?
questions.solution_markdown:
It's almost exact. Notice that this homogeneous of degree 1. The trick is to set $y=x v$ as a substitution for $y$. Hence, $d y=v d x+x d v$ and $v=y / x$. In particular,

$$
x^{2} d v+\left(2 x v-x e^{x}\right) d x
$$

which is now exact! Hence, our solution is: $x^{2} v-x e^{x}+e^{x}=C \rightsquigarrow x y-x e^{x}+e^{x}=C$. Substituting $(1,0)$ gives us $C=0$. Hence, we $2 y-e^{2}=0$ and so $e^{2} / 2$.

questions.question_markdown:
Let $y=f(x)$ be a solution of the equation:

$$
y^{\prime}+\frac{y}{x}=\sin x
$$

such that $f(\pi)=1$. What is the value $f(\pi / 2)$ ?
questions.solution_markdown:
Integration factor is $x$ and then use integration by parts to get:

$$
-\cos (x)+\frac{1}{x} \cdot \sin x+C=y
$$

Then we see that $C=0$ from the initial condition and so $f(\pi / 2)=\frac{2}{\pi}$
